parse.why: For HCGPE, the DSC curve shows no thermally induced phase transitions, indicating it is totally amorphous; the glass transition is not obvious (Results & Discussion; Figure S3). No BET/porosity metrics, acidity values, crystal sizes, or Si/Al ratios are reported for HCGPE.

For the high-concentration electrolyte (HCE), the text reports only electrochemical parameters (e.g., activation energy 0.18 eV; tLi+ = 0.48; lower onset oxidation potential than HCGPE) and provides no data on morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, or porosity/acidity (BET surface area, pore volumes, or acid site amounts). Therefore, all schema fields are null for HCE.

Fig. 2b: "This LAWTP sample shows an irregular particle morphology with particle sizes ranging from several hundred nanometers to several micrometers." No BET surface area, pore volume, or acidity values are reported for LAWTP; structural (XRD), valence (XPS), and composition (ICP) data are provided without quantitative porosity or acidity metrics.

Fig. 3(a)-(b) are photographs of the PVDF-HFP-SN and PVDF-HFP-SN-LAWTP solid electrolytes; Fig. 3b demonstrates the excellent flexibility of these SCEs. No BET surface area, pore volume, acidity, or crystal size values are reported for PVDF-HFP-SN.

characterization.morphology: LAWTP particles distributed in the PVDF-HFP polymer matrix with some non-penetrating pores; flexible membrane

parse.why: Applies to all PVDF-HFP-SN-LAWTP solid electrolytes including the 0.05 g sample: 'the LAWTP particles are distributed in the PVDF-HFP polymer matrix and some non-penetrating pores can be observed.' Fig. 3b demonstrates the excellent flexibility of these SCEs. No BET/porosity, acidity, or crystal size values were reported for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.05LAWTP.

Applies to PVDF-HFP-SN-LAWTP SCEs (including the 0.1LAWTP sample): “Photographs and SEM images of these SCEs are displayed in Fig. 3… the LAWTP particles are distributed in the PVDF-HFP polymer matrix and some non-penetrating pores can be observed. The cross-sectional image shows a uniform thickness of about 120 μm.” No BET surface area, pore volume, Si/Al ratio, or acidity (NH3-TPD/Py-IR) data were reported for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP.

No BET/porosity, acidity, crystal size, or definitive morphology descriptors are reported specifically for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.2LAWTP. Numerical values in the text are provided for PVDF-HFP-SN-0.1LAWTP (e.g., ionic conductivity, tLi+, DSC, LSV) or generally for PVDF-HFP-SN-LAWTP membranes (e.g., ~120 μm thickness) without attribution to the 0.2LAWTP sample. Therefore, all requested properties are unavailable for the specified material.

For neat [HMG][FSI], the paper reports thermal transitions (e.g., phase transitions at 8 °C and 67 °C and melting at 87 °C; low entropy of fusion 7 J mol−1 K−1) and electrochemical properties (∼8 V window at 75 °C), as well as transport (conductivity, NMR diffusion), but provides no morphology (SEM/TEM), no crystal size, no porosity (BET/N2 sorption), and no acidity (NH3-TPD/pyridine-IR) data. See sections 4.1 (DSC) and 4.5 (CV). No BET/porosity values or acidity measures are presented anywhere; only synthesis notes include impurity K content (380 ppm), which is outside the schema.

Results (DSC): "The thermograms reveal that the systems are fully miscible with a single-phase morphology" for the investigated solid binary (PMMA)0.91-(PIL)0.09 samples, which includes (PMMA)0.91-(IM14)0.09. No BET/porosity, acidity, or crystallite size values specific to (PMMA)0.91-(IM14)0.09 are reported in the provided text.

DSC thermograms reveal that the systems are fully miscible with a single-phase morphology (Fig. S1, magnified in Fig. 4a) for all investigated solid binary polymeric (PMMA)0.91-(PIL)0.09 samples, which includes (PMMA)0.91-(TFSI)0.09. Table 1 reports for (PMMA)0.91-(TFSI)0.09: Tg = 64 °C; Td1 = 298 °C (w1 = 9%); Td2 = 394 °C (w2 = 65%); Td3 = 495 °C (w3 = 25%); wPIL = 30%.

DSC thermograms of the solid binary polymeric (PMMA)0.91-(PIL)0.09 samples reveal the systems are fully miscible with a single-phase morphology (Section 3.1: “The thermograms reveal that the systems are fully miscible with a single-phase morphology…”). No BET/porosity, crystal size, or acidity values are reported for (PMMA)0.91-(TFO)0.09; only thermal (Tg, TGA) and NMR data are provided.

For (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40, the paper reports that the PMMA addition increased viscosity but the blends "did not form free-standing gels, leading to their classification as IM14-gel-like systems" (Section 3.3), hence morphology = gel-like. The quantitative results provided for this sample are PFG-NMR self-diffusion coefficients at 40/55/70 °C (Table 2; Fig. 7), which are outside the requested schema. No BET surface area, pore volumes, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, or acidity amounts are reported for this sample; therefore those fields are set to null.

The sample is explicitly described as one of two IM14-gel-like systems: "Two IM14-gellike samples, (DBUH-IM14)0.60-(PMMA)0.40 and (DBUH-IM14)0.55(Li)0.06-(PMMA)0.39, were obtained..." and "the blended systems did not form free-standing gels, leading to their classification as IM14-gel-like systems." No BET/porosity, acidity, crystal size, or Si/Al ratio values are reported for this sample.

For M1 (Pentafluorophenylacrylate), the paper reports NMR data, boiling point (27 °C at 0.05 mbar), and refractive index (n_D^25 = 1.4318), but provides no morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, porosity (BET, pore volumes), or acidity values relevant to the schema. Thus all fields are null.

For M2 (Pentafluorophenylmethacrylate), the paper provides boiling point, refractive index, and NMR data (e.g., “colorless liquid … boiling point of 30°C at 0.03 mbar … n_D^25 = 1.4397 … 1H NMR … 19F NMR”), and qualitative solubility/stability, but no morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area, pore volumes, or Brønsted/Lewis acidity values. Therefore all schema fields are null.

For PM1, the paper reports NMR, IR (1782 cm-1), elemental analysis, GPC (Mn 24050 g/mol, Mw 37010 g/mol, PDI 1.5), solubility, and reaction conversions/kinetics. It does not provide morphology (shape), crystal size, porosity (BET or pore volumes), acidity (Bronsted/Lewis), or Si/Al ratios; therefore all fields are set to null.

For PM2 (Poly(pentafluorophenylmethacrylate)), the paper reports NMR, IR, elemental analysis, and GPC molecular weights but provides no morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, porosity (BET surface area or pore volumes), or acidity data.
Evidence specific to PM2:
- IR: 1780.2 cm^-1 (C=O)
- Elemental analysis: C calc. 47.64%, found 47.94%; H calc. 2.00%, found 2.19%
- GPC data: Mn = 26,680 g/mol; Mw = 48,830 g/mol; PDI = 1.6
- Solubility: highly soluble in many organic solvents; insoluble in n-hexane or methanol
None of these map to the schema fields (morphology shape, crystal size in nm, Si/Al ratio, BET m2/g, pore volumes cm3/g, or acid amounts). Therefore all fields are null.

The paper states: "The PFPE_E10 polymers are thermally stable ... amorphous liquids over a broad temperature range" (Results), which applies to PFPE_E10-DMC. No BET/porosity, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, or acidity values were reported for PFPE_E10-DMC; only thermal (e.g., Tg = -92 °C; Td(5%) = 194 °C in Table 2) and salt-solubility data, which are outside the schema.

For XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.3: (1) Fig. 2 presents only AC impedance for this specific composition; no morphology, porosity, or acidity values are given. (2) Morphology (STEM/EDS) is reported for XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.35 and XL-PFPE_0.40 (Fig. 3, Fig. 4), not for XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.3. (3) No BET/porosity or acid site data are provided anywhere. (4) TGA (Fig. 6) gives thermal stability for XL-POSS0.12-PFPE_0.30, but thermal parameters are outside the defined schema fields.

Morphology: STEM was performed on two samples, one cited as “XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35” in the text, but the figure caption and detailed discussion consistently use “XL-POSS0.12PFPE0.35” for heterogeneity and EDS results (Fig. 3b, Fig. 4b). Due to this inconsistency, morphology specific to XL-POSS0.13PFPE0.35 cannot be asserted with confidence. Crystal size/porosity/acidity/Si/Al ratio: no numerical or qualitative results relevant to these properties were reported for this sample.

“The STEM image of XL-PFPE_0.40 (Fig. 3a) is featureless, suggesting that the sample is homogeneous.” “EDS elemental maps … show a uniform distribution of these elements throughout the samples. The absence of large sulfur rich domains indicates that the salt is uniformly distributed in the electrolyte.”

For PFP (PETMP–TFEMA–PEGDA), the paper provides FT-IR and NMR confirmation of structure and a GPC number-average molecular weight ≈ 26,300, and mentions electrochemical stability (~4.7 V), but these properties are not represented in the given schema. No BET surface area, pore volumes, acidity metrics, morphology, or crystal size are reported specifically for neat PFP. Reported SEM/EDS, DSC, crystallinity, conductivity, and transference number pertain to PEO–PFP composites (e.g., 80PEO20PFP) rather than pure PFP, so corresponding schema fields are set to null.

PEP (PETMP-EMA-PEGDA) is only briefly mentioned: “As a contrast, the same synthetic procedure was adopted for PETMP-EMA-PEGDA (PEP)…” and “the tLi+ of the 80PEO20PEP electrolyte was also tested and found to be approximately 0.18 (Fig. S5)”. No BET/porosity, acidity, morphology, or crystal size values are reported for PEP; thus all schema fields are null.

For FR-PU, the paper reports flame-retardancy metrics (LOI 27.6%, Tp 407.9 °C, PHRR 18.8 W g−1, THR 2.8 kJ g−1, ID/IG 1.158; Table 1), thermal/mechanical properties (e.g., Td,5% 292.5 °C, Tg −37.8 °C, tensile strength 1.26 MPa; Table 2), and electrochemical data, but provides no BET surface area, pore volumes, acidity parameters, morphology descriptors, or crystal size values for FR-PU. Hence all schema fields are null.

Only flame-retardancy metrics are reported for Pure PU (Table 1: LOI 16.8%, Tp 402.4 °C, PHRR 55.4 W/g, THR 6.1 kJ/g, ID/IG 2.137). No morphology, porosity (BET or pore volumes), crystal size, or acidity data are provided for Pure PU in the provided text; thus all schema fields are set to null.

For FR-PU-5% Li, only thermal/mechanical data are reported (Table 2: Td,5% 284.3 °C, residue char 2.32%, Tg -33.6 °C, tensile strength 1.41 MPa, elongation 191.1%). No morphology (e.g., particle shape), crystal size, BET surface area/porosity, or acidity (Brønsted/Lewis) values are provided anywhere in the text, figures, or tables; thus all schema fields are null.

For FR-PU-10% Li (Table 2), the paper reports only thermal/mechanical data: Td,5% = 272.7 °C; residue char = 6.82%; Tg = −25.2 °C; tensile strength = 2.01 MPa; elongation at break = 171.4%. No sample-specific morphology description, crystal size, porosity (BET/volumes), or acidity (Brønsted/Lewis amounts or L/B ratio) is provided. Methods list FTIR, TGA, DSC, mechanical tests, EIS, LSV/CV; no BET or acidity quantification is reported.

For 'FR-PU-15% Li', the paper reports only thermal and mechanical data (Table 2: Td,5% = 267.4 °C; residue char = 12.09%; Tg = −20.3 °C; tensile strength = 2.26 MPa; elongation at break = 139.6%). No morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, porosity (BET or pore volumes), or acidity values are provided for this specific sample.

For FR-PU-20% Li, the article provides thermal/mechanical properties (Table 2), ionic conductivity and electrochemical stability window (Fig. 7, Table 3), and cell performance (Fig. 8). It does not report morphology (e.g., sheet/spherical), crystal size, Si/Al ratio, nitrogen sorption/BET porosity, or quantitative acidity (e.g., NH3-TPD or pyridine-IR). Therefore all schema fields are set to null.

For FR-PU-25% Li, only thermal/mechanical properties are reported (Table 2: Td,5% = 221.1 °C; residue char 9.62%; Tg −11.4 °C; tensile strength 2.62 MPa; elongation 111.9%), which are outside the schema. No BET surface area, pore volumes, acidity metrics, crystallite sizes, or specific morphology descriptions for FR-PU-25% Li are provided; the photo and several electrochemical details pertain to FR-PU-20% Li. Hence all schema fields are null.

Ta-ESM shows a fibrous morphology: 'The obtained fibrous nature of the membrane...' (Fig. 1). A porosity of 64.1% is reported for Ta-ESM, but no BET surface area or pore volume values are provided. No crystal size is given for Ta-ESM (only LLZTO particle size), and there is no acidity or Si/Al ratio information.

Morphology: text states both ESM and Ta-ESM membranes show a smooth, fibrous nature (Fig. 1; “The obtained fibrous nature of the membrane...”). No BET surface area or pore volumes reported for ESM; only overall membrane porosity of 58.2% (not compatible with required BET/volume units), so porosity fields set to null. No acidity or Si/Al ratio data for ESM.

For LDFBDB, the paper provides TGA onset (decomposition starts at 170 °C), solubility in PC (0.518 mol dm^-3 at 25 °C), ionic conductivities of 0.10 mol dm^-3 solutions (e.g., 0.414 mS cm^-1 in PC; 2.167 in PC+DME; 0.960 in PC+EC+EMC; 1.124 in PC+EMC), and oxidation potential (~3.7 V vs Li+/Li). However, there are no results reported for morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area or pore volumes, or acidity parameters. Therefore these fields are null.

For FLDFBDB, the paper reports properties such as thermal decomposition onset at 185 °C (Fig. 2), solubility in PC S=0.443 mol dm-3 with corresponding conductivity 1.88 mS cm-1 at 25 °C (Table 1), conductivities at 0.10 mol dm-3 in various solvents e.g., PC 1.06 mS cm-1 and PC+DME 3.46 mS cm-1 (Table 2), and oxidation potential ~3.9 V vs Li+/Li (Fig. 4). However, no morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area or pore volumes, or acidity values are provided for FLDFBDB; therefore all schema fields are null.

For FLBDOB (lithium [3-fluoro-1,2-benzenediolato(2-)o,o' oxalato]borate), the paper provides decomposition temperature (262 °C in N2, Fig. 2), solubility in PC (0.275 mol dm−3 at 25 °C; Table 1), conductivities (e.g., 5.60 mS cm−1 in PC at saturation; Table 1), and oxidation potential (~4.0 V vs Li+/Li; Fig. 4). None of these map to the schema fields (morphology, crystal size, BET surface area or pore volumes, or acidity site amounts). No relevant morphology, crystal size, porosity (BET/volumes), or acidity (Bronsted/Lewis amounts or L/B ratio) data are reported for FLBDOB; therefore all fields are null.

For LBOB (lithium bis(oxalate)borate), the paper reports thermal decomposition onset (~302 °C, Fig. 2), solubility in PC (0.362 mol dm^-3, Table 1), ionic conductivities at 0.1 mol dm^-3 (e.g., 5.56 mS cm^-1 in PC, 6.79 mS cm^-1 in PC+DME, Table 2), and oxidation potential (~4.5 V vs Li+/Li, Fig. 4). However, it provides no morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area or pore volumes, nor Brønsted/Lewis acidity data for LBOB. Therefore all schema fields are set to null.

AOEM2 is described as 'obtained as a colorless liquid ... by distillation under reduced pressure (b.p.: 46 °C/3 mmHg)'. No measurements relevant to crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area or pore volumes, or acidity (Bronsted/Lewis) are reported for AOEM2.

For MFSM2, the paper provides viscosity (2.0 mPa s at 25°C), dielectric constant (7.3), ionic conductivity of 1M LiTFSI solution (1.4 mS cm-1), Tg (−118°C), and electrochemical window values (reductive 1.0 V, oxidative 4.5 V) in Table 1. There is no reported morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, porosity (BET surface area or pore volumes), or acidity (Brønsted/Lewis amounts or ratio) specific to MFSM2; therefore all schema fields are null.

For DFSM2, the paper reports viscosity (1.8 mPa s at 25 C), dielectric constant (9.5), ionic conductivity (1.8 mS cm-1 with 1 M LiTFSI), Tg (-109 C), and redox potentials (reduction 1.2 V, oxidation 5.0 V), plus NMR and that DFSM2 is a colorless liquid. No data are provided for morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area, pore volumes, or acidity (Bronsted/Lewis). Therefore all schema fields are null.

E0 is a liquid electrolyte: 'E0 1M LiPF6 EC/EMC (3/7 in vol.)'. The paper reports electrochemical/thermal performance but provides no morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area, pore volumes, or acidity values for E0; therefore all fields are null.

E1 is defined as an electrolyte formulation: "E1 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.)". Reported results for E1 concern electrochemical performance (e.g., "For E1 ... the discharge and charge capacity at the first cycle is 332.4 and 455.7 mAh g−1, respectively, and the initial coulombic efficiency (ICE) is 72.9%."). The paper provides no morphology, crystal size, BET surface area or pore volumes, acidity parameters, or Si/Al ratio for E1; thus all schema fields are null.

E2 is defined as an electrolyte formulation: 'E2 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.) + 5 wt % VC'. The paper reports viscosity, dielectric constant, ionic conductivity, electrochemical windows, DSC thermal behavior, and cycling performance, but provides no morphology, crystal size, BET surface area/porosity, or acidity values for E2; therefore all fields are set to null.

E3 is defined as: "E3 1M LiPF6 EC/DFSM2/EMC (2/3/5 in vol.) + 5 wt % FEC". The reported quantified physical properties (viscosity, dielectric constant, Tg, conductivity, oxidative/reductive potentials) are for individual solvents MFSM2/DFSM2 or their 1 M LiTFSI solutions (Table 1), not for the E3 electrolyte mixture. There are no data for morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area, pore volumes, or acidity for E3. Thermal stability figures involve systems with electrodes rather than standalone E3: "DSC profiles of E0 electrolyte and E3 electrolyte (a) with lithiated graphite and (b) with 4.4 V charged Li_xCoO2 cathode." Therefore, all schema fields are set to null due to lack of directly applicable values for E3.

For 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE (3:7 vol.), the paper reports oxidation stability (~5.08 V vs Li/Li+ by LSV, Fig. 2a) and transport properties (tLi+ = 0.352 experimental; DPF6− = 2.885×10^-6 cm2/s, DLi+ = 1.782×10^-6 cm2/s from Table 2) and a solvation structure Li+(FEC)2PF6− (coordination number 4). However, no morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, BET surface area/porosity, or acidity parameters (Brønsted/Lewis, amounts or L/B ratio) are provided for this liquid electrolyte; thus those fields are null.

For the sample '1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC (3:5:2, by vol.)', the paper reports electrochemical/transport data rather than morphology, crystal size, Si/Al ratio, porosity, or acidity. Examples: oxidation stability extended to ~5.31 V vs Li/Li+ (Fig. 2a: “the value of 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC (3:5:2, by vol.) can be extended to ~5.31 V”), Li+ transference number 0.426 (Table 2 and text: “The calculated t_Li+ … were 0.426 for 1 M LiPF6 in FEC/TTE/EMC”), and diffusion coefficients (Table 2). These parameters are outside the provided schema, and no morphology/crystal size/porosity/acidity values are given for this electrolyte; therefore all schema fields are null.

For FBCP_20, Table 1 reports Mw=43100 g/mol, PDI=1.35, wPFMA=20%, and Tm=56.3 °C, but the schema has no fields for these. No BET surface area, pore volume, or acidity data are provided. Morphology and structural descriptions (SEM/AFM/TEM/SAXS) are reported for salt-doped electrolyte membranes (FBCPE_20 or doped FBCPs), not the neat FBCP_20; thus they are not assigned to FBCP_20. Consequently, all schema-requested fields remain null.

Only numerical/definitive properties for FBCP_30 found are Mw/PDI/wPFMA and Tm from Table 1 ("FBCP_30: Mw 50200 g/mol, PDI 1.23, wPFMA 30%, Tm 60.8 °C"), which are outside the provided schema fields. No porosity, acidity, or crystal size data are reported for FBCP_30. Morphological characterizations (SEM/AFM/TEM/SAXS) are performed on FBCPEs (LiTFSI-doped electrolytes) rather than neat FBCP_30 (e.g., "The thin film morphologies of FBCPs mixed with LiTFSI ([EO]/[Li] = 20:1) were evaluated..." and "Fig. 4 shows the SAXS diffractograms of FBCPs doped with LiTFSI"), so morphology is not assigned to FBCP_30. Mechanical data for FBCP_30 (e.g., "The tensile fracture strain of FBCP_30 can reach 440%") also fall outside the schema.

For FBCP_40, Table 1 provides Mw=58600 g/mol, PDI=1.42, wPFMA=40%, and Tm=59.5 °C, but these are not fields in the schema. Morphology and microphase information (SEM/AFM/TEM/SAXS) are reported for LiTFSI-doped membranes labeled FBCPE_40, not the neat polymer FBCP_40. No BET surface area, pore volume, acidity, or crystal size data specific to FBCP_40 are provided. Therefore all schema fields are set to null.

DPPA0 is defined as the GPE without TFOA. The Results state: 'The polymer electrolyte composed of DPPA without TFOP modifier exhibits a highly ordered polymer network structure, resulting in a certain degree of crystallinity that impedes the migration of Li ions.' For DPPA0, a Tg step at 15.2°C is reported, but no BET/porosity, crystal size, or acidity values are provided.

No DPPA1-specific physical properties (morphology, crystal size, BET surface area/porosity, acidity) with numerical or definitive results are reported. Quantitative data given are mainly for DPPA3 (e.g., conductivity 2.52×10^-3 S/cm, t_Li+ = 0.61, decomposition voltage 4.81 V) or DPPA0 (e.g., t_Li+ = 0.31, decomposition voltage 4.63 V), not DPPA1. Other characterizations (FTIR, XRD, SEM, DSC/Tg, TGA) are discussed generally for “DPPA-TFOA-PAN electrolyte” or specific to DPPA3, without specifying DPPA1; therefore, we cannot attribute values to DPPA1.

DPPA2 refers to the GPE with DPPA:TFOA=2:1. In the provided text, explicit numerical properties (e.g., ionic conductivity, t_Li+, decomposition voltage, cycling stability) are only reported for DPPA3, while figures show trends for multiple ratios without tabulated values or figure-caption numbers for DPPA2. General statements such as “all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower impedance and higher ionic conductivity than pure DPPA” apply to DPPA2 but lack quantitative values. No DPPA2-specific morphology, porosity, crystal size, or acidity values are given. Therefore, all fields are set to null per the rules.

For DPPA3 (DPPA:TFOA=3:1), the paper reports electrochemical properties but no applicable quantitative data for the requested fields. Evidence: "DPPA3 exhibits the most outstanding results, reaching 2.52 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30°C"; "the Li+ of DPPA3 significantly increases to 0.61"; "The decomposition voltage of the DPPA3 ... is 4.81 V"; XRD indicates amorphous character: "The emergence of the wide peak suggests that ... the polymer electrolyte shows an amorphous structure"; DSC: "TFOA modified DPPA3 GPEs does not exhibit any glass transition within −40~90°C." No numerical data are provided for morphology (shape), crystal size (nm), Si/Al ratio, BET surface area (m2/g), pore volumes (cm3/g), or acidity (mmol/g), so these fields are set to null.

DPPA4 (DPPA:TFOA=4:1) is defined in Section 2.4, but specific physicochemical results are only reported for DPPA3 and DPPA0 (e.g., conductivity 2.52×10^-3 S/cm and t_Li+ = 0.61 for DPPA3). The text provides general trends for “all samples containing TFOA” (lower impedance, higher conductivity than pure DPPA) without DPPA4-specific values. No BET/porosity, acidity, crystal size, or explicit morphology specific to DPPA4 is given in the provided excerpts. Therefore all fields are set to null per '宁缺毋滥'. Relevant quotes: “DPPA2, DPPA3, DPPA4 and DPPA5 were prepared with ratios of 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 and 5:1 respectively.” and “all samples containing TFOA demonstrated lower electrochemical impedance and higher ionic conductivity than that of the pure DPPA GPEs… DPPA3 exhibits the most outstanding results…”

DPPA5 is defined as DPPA:TFOA = 5:1 (Section 2.4). However, the Results provide specific numeric data only for DPPA3 (e.g., conductivity 2.52×10^-3 S/cm and t_Li+ = 0.61 at 30 °C; decomposition voltage 4.81 V) and general statements for all TFOA-containing samples. No DPPA5-specific morphology, crystallinity size, BET/porosity, or acidity values are reported in the provided text or figure captions. Therefore all fields are set to null.

SEM discussion (applies to all FG-GPE samples): 'Fig. 1c~d and S1 depict the surface morphology of FG-GPE. It is evident that all pores have disappeared due to polymer polymerization and liquid filling, resulting in a smoother and pore-free electrolyte surface.' No BET surface area, pore volume, crystal size, or acidity values are reported for FG-GPE-0; other quantitative results (e.g., ESW 4.54 V, ionic conductivity 3.42 × 10^-4 S cm^-1) are outside this schema.

SEM (Fig. 1c) for FG-GPE-3 shows that all pores disappeared after polymerization and liquid filling, yielding a smoother, pore-free electrolyte surface. No BET/porosity or acidity values were reported for FG-GPE-3. Other reported properties for FG-GPE-3 include ESW 4.55 V, ionic conductivity 2.67×10^-3 S cm^-1 at 30 °C, and t_Li+ = 0.55, but these are outside the provided schema.

characterization.morphology: Amorphous (non-crystalline) at room temperature; smooth, pore-free electrolyte surface after polymerization/liquid filling

parse.why: Evidence for FG-GPE-5 morphology: "It is evident that all pores have disappeared due to polymer polymerization and liquid filling, resulting in a smoother and pore-free electrolyte surface" (Fig. 1c–d, includes FG-GPE-5). DSC: "FG-GPE-3 and FG-GPE-5 exist in an amorphous non-crystalline state at room temperature" (Fig. S12b). Other measured properties for FG-GPE-5 that do not fit the schema: ionic conductivity at 30 °C = 4.74 × 10^-3 S cm^-1 (Fig. 2e); Li+ transference number t_Li+ = 0.68 (Fig. S9). No BET/porosity metrics (m2/g, cm3/g) or acidity values reported; therefore set to null.

Results state for FG-GPE samples: “Fig. 1c~d and S1 depict the surface morphology of FG-GPE. It is evident that all pores have disappeared due to polymer polymerization and liquid filling, resulting in a smoother and pore-free electrolyte surface,” which applies to all FG-GPE variants including FG-GPE-7. No BET/porosity, crystal size, or acidity values are reported for FG-GPE-7.

For ILPE-EMImTFSI, the paper reports ionic conductivity (5.3×10^-3 S/cm at 30 °C), Raman-derived Li+-coordinated TFSI fraction (32%), anodic stability (~5.5 V vs Li), and thermal degradation behavior (~400–440 °C), but the provided schema does not include fields for these properties. No BET surface area or pore volume data are reported (Fig. 1 is conductivity; Fig. 2 Raman; Fig. 4 LSV; Fig. 5 TGA). The SEM/size (~800 nm fiber diameter) pertains to the electrospun PI membrane prior to IL impregnation (Fig. 1 inset) and is not explicitly assigned to the ILPE-EMImTFSI after soaking; thus morphology is not confidently attributable. No crystal size, Si/Al ratio, or acidity (Brønsted/Lewis) data are provided.

characterization.morphology: leaf-vein-like nanofiber membrane with MnO2 nanosheets coating

Like PEO, PPC is immiscible with PVDF-HFP, resulting in a phase-separated PPC-LPIFD (Supplementary Fig. 45). In addition, the reduction of polycarbonate forms an organic-rich SEI compared to PEO-LPIFD. As a result, the PPC-LPIFD has the lowest CCD (0.3 mA cm^-2) and CE (<90%) (Supplementary Fig. 45).

PEO-LPIFD shows phase separation (Supplementary Fig. 10), with S elements (LiFSI) present more in the O-rich domain (PEO-rich), consistent with the immiscibility of PEO and PVDF-HFP. STXM spectra also show immiscibility between PEO and PVDF-HFP, with distinct PEO-rich and PVDF-HFP-rich regions (Fig. 3a,d).
